name: Yamato Takeru
description: Yamato Takeru, a legendary Japanese prince of the Yamato dynasty, is celebrated as a heroic figure who embodied courage, loyalty, and military prowess. Born as Prince Ousu, he earned the name Yamato Takeru, meaning "The Brave of Yamato," after successfully subduing the Kumaso brothers in Kyushu with cunning and bravery. His father, Emperor Keikō, often tasked him with dangerous missions to quell rebellions and expand the Yamato court's influence. Yamato Takeru's journeys took him across Japan, where he faced treacherous landscapes, formidable enemies, and supernatural beings. He is known for his victories against rebellious tribes, his encounters with deities, and his contributions to the unification of Japan. Despite his heroic deeds, Yamato Takeru's life was marked by tragedy and loneliness. He often felt used by his father and longed for recognition and peace. He met his demise after a battle where he was poisoned by a curse, lamenting his fate and longing for his homeland. His legend has been immortalized in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, shaping Japanese national identity and serving as an inspiration for warriors and leaders throughout history. He represents the ideals of imperial expansion, warrior spirit, and tragic heroism, making him a central figure in Japanese mythology and cultural heritage.
subtitle: The Brave Prince of Yamato

keyMyths.title: Subduing the Kumaso
keyMyths.description: Yamato Takeru, disguised as a woman, infiltrated the Kumaso brothers' celebration and assassinated them, earning him the title 'Yamato Takeru'. This act demonstrated his cunning and bravery, solidifying his reputation as a formidable warrior.

keyMyths.title: The Grass-Cutting Sword
keyMyths.description: During a battle, Yamato Takeru was surrounded by fire, but he used the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi to cut away the grass and escape the flames. This legendary sword became a symbol of his protection and power.

keyMyths.title: Otachibana-hime's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: To calm a stormy sea, Yamato Takeru's wife, Otachibana-hime, sacrificed herself by throwing herself into the ocean. Her sacrifice allowed the prince to safely cross the sea and continue his mission.

sources.author: Ō no Yasumaro
sources.description: The oldest extant chronicle of Japan, containing myths, legends, and historical accounts.
sources.text: The oldest extant chronicle of Japan, containing myths, legends, and historical accounts.

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.description: The second-oldest book of classical Japanese history, more detailed and elaborate than the Kojiki.
